title: Offices of Real Estate Agents and Brokers

narrative.executive_view: This is a weak AI-value acquisition despite meaningful workflow automation. The supplied wage base is small relative to gross commissions, while agent splits are contractual pass-throughs rather than controllable payroll. AI can make staff and agents more productive, but brokerages have fragile retention of both agents and leads, and post-settlement compensation remains in flux [S1].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI is already material in listing copy, marketing, research, follow-up and transaction administration: S1 reports 20% daily and 22% weekly AI use among surveyed REALTORS®, while 46% reported no noticeable business impact [S1]. We treat the 42% regular-use total, rather than all reported experimentation, as a proxy for effective operating adoption. AI can compress clerical and drafting work, not eliminate licensed negotiation, supervision, local judgment or trust in a high-stakes transaction. The supplied 3-digit role mix is contaminated by property-management and lessor jobs, so its physical-work shares are deliberately assigned low automation.
narrative.value_capture: A brokerage retains only its company dollar after paying agent splits; productivity in an agent's personal workflow does not automatically enlarge that retained share. Technology can support pricing and service, but digital lead sources and MLS/portal channels limit office-level bargaining power. Consequently savings are likely shared with agents, spent on lead generation, or competed away; pi_dist and pi_moat are both deliberately low rather than treating commission pass-through as labor savings [S1].
narrative.adoption_timing: S1 reports 20% daily and 22% weekly AI use; adding those source-stated frequencies yields 42% regular use [S1]. This is below the 50% effective-adoption boundary, while the additional 27% using AI a few times monthly and 46% reporting no noticeable business impact show that experimentation should not be equated with effective workflow change. A two-year estimate to 50% effective adoption is therefore a bounded judgment, not a source-reported forecast.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The deterministic screen identifies only 259 firms in the indicated EBITDA band and warns that firm counts are dominated by very small shells. Acquisition economics are therefore thin and heterogeneous: agent books can move, owner compensation complicates EBITDA normalization, and retained-company-dollar economics cannot be compared with gross commission revenue. We estimate a handful of active strategic consolidators but do not use that fact as AI-adoption evidence.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid representation is pressured, not extinguished. NAR's 2025 buyer/seller survey reports 88% of buyers and 91% of sellers using an agent or broker, chiefly for negotiation, explanation, marketing and pricing [S2]. Those services survive AI, but AI reduces the information and paperwork component and makes a lower-fee licensed-execution model more plausible; this terminal-demand conclusion is separate from capture.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The key uncertainty is the mismatch between the 531 proxy occupation mix and a brokerage's actual payroll, compounded by rapidly changing compensation practices. NAR sources represent member respondents, not all brokerages, and regular survey use is only a proxy for effective operating adoption. The model also cannot infer a durable brokerage moat from consumer use of agents; portable agents, portals and franchise arrangements can absorb the value.

sources.title: REALTORS Embrace AI, Digital Tools to Enhance Client Service, NAR Survey Finds
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 REALTOR AI-use frequency (20% daily, 22% weekly, 27% a few times monthly, 32% never), reported business impact, technology use and brokerage support; member-survey scope.

sources.title: Top 10 Takeaways from NAR's 2025 Profile of Home Buyers and Sellers
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 buyer and seller use of agents and the functions buyers and sellers seek.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Task estimates use the supplied 3-digit proxy only as a constrained wage-weighting device; AI evidence supports office and marketing automation, while physical and licensed work remains partly human.
caveats: The supplied 3-digit 531 mix is materially contaminated by lessors and property managers, not brokerage offices.
Independent-contractor agent commissions are outside W-2 payroll and not included as automatable labor.

role_judgments.rationale: Physical maintenance is a sibling-code proxy, with only scheduling/documentation automated.
Property-management proxy; reporting automates but field supervision and tenant work remain.
Listing, marketing and scheduling automate; licensed relationship and negotiation do not.
Routine inquiries and booking automate; in-person exceptions remain.
Listing administration and transaction coordination are document-heavy.
Reporting and recruiting assistance automate; broker supervision remains.
Cleaning is predominantly physical; routing is the limited AI task.
Scheduling, correspondence and document preparation automate substantially.
Reconciliation and commission-disbursement preparation are automatable with review.
Physical-work supervision proxy has limited reporting automation.
Unlisted brokerage marketing and support work is mixed and partly automatable.

pi_dist.rationale: Office savings face agent-split pass-through, technology/lead spending and competition; the reported neutral AI-impact share supports cautious retention.
caveats: Commission terms and post-settlement repricing vary by market and brokerage.

pi_moat.rationale: Consumers still use agents, but portable agent relationships and externally supplied technology constrain a brokerage-office moat.
caveats: NAR member evidence measures agent use, not ownership of lead flow or retention of an acquired office.

t50_years.rationale: The calculated 42% regular-use proxy is below 50%; the 27% monthly-use group is a plausible near-term conversion pool, but S1's 46% no-noticeable-impact result means effective workflow adoption is not yet complete.
caveats: This is an estimate of effective operating adoption, not a source-reported forecast; regular REALTOR use is not identical to NAICS 531210 brokerage payroll automation.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: S1 reports 20% daily and 22% weekly AI use. Their sum is selected as a transparent regular-use proxy for effective operating adoption; occasional monthly use is not counted as effective adoption.
current_adoption_pct.plausible_range: 20%-69% depending on whether daily-only or any reported use is the effective-use threshold
caveats: S1 does not label any frequency tier as effective adoption, and its REALTOR respondent scope is not identical to NAICS 531210 firms.

historical_analogs.value: eSignature and CRM adoption: digital tools diffuse before changing broker economics.
historical_analogs.rationale: NAR reports mature eSignature use alongside newer AI use, supporting a staged workflow-adoption analogue.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Workflow tools may diffuse faster or slower than AI.
caveats: Analogue is directional, not a causal forecast.

selection.rationale: A conservative owner-age proxy for an older, experienced agent population; no code-specific broker-owner statistic was reopened.
caveats: This is not a measured NAICS 531210 owner statistic.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documented a code-specific succession shortage, so it is not credited.
caveats: Absence of reopened documentation is not evidence that no succession issue exists.

active_consolidators.rationale: A small active-consolidator estimate reflects a fragmented brokerage market but is not used outside buyability.
caveats: No code-specific, current consolidator census was reopened.

buy_mult.rationale: Low multiple estimate reflects fragile retained-company-dollar economics and portable agent books.
caveats: No reopened transaction-comparable source; broker-owner normalization and revenue basis vary.

exit_mult.rationale: Modest exit estimate assumes only a slight platform/scale premium over a small-office purchase.
caveats: No reopened transaction-comparable source; not a claim of observed market pricing.

pricing_structure.value: Percentage commission with agent-split pass-through; compensation is negotiated and in flux.
pricing_structure.rationale: NAR describes agent-mediated transaction services while its technology data shows evolving workflows; this is a structural characterization.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Traditional split, capped-fee and virtual brokerage models coexist.
caveats: No single broker P&L or post-settlement commission series is used; gross commission and retained company dollar must not be conflated.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The regular-use proxy is below 50%, and the sizable monthly-use group supports a short but positive estimate to effective adoption; moderate task automation and a two-year t50 are consistent.

terminal_value.justification: High consumer agent use supports continuing paid demand, but AI reduces information and paperwork value and pushes toward lower-fee execution [S2].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Consolidator and multiple estimates are confined to B and M; no deal evidence is used for V, C, A or terminal demand.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Commission negotiation and technology-enabled service substitution are reflected only in pi_dist/pi_moat, not labor replaceability.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 3-digit 531 occupation mix includes material lessor/property-management contamination and is not a brokerage-only wage distribution.
sensitivity: A brokerage-specific mix could raise office-support share but excludes physical proxy roles; direction of aggregate V is uncertain.

market_boundary.selection_basis: NAICS title; proxy contamination is excluded from thesis interpretation.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10 to 2.5 across the stated range.
V is sensitive to the non-brokerage proxy composition.

reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates 2026-07-21_gpt-5.6-terra_v312p1_531210: the unsupported observed 41% claim is replaced by an explicit calculation from S1's daily and weekly use frequencies, with t50 reassessed against that effective-adoption proxy.
